ontology.foodex2_description: Mutton, cooked, stewed/simmered — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for spiced mutton soup
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Sup Kambing; A0CKM (mutton, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. Spice blend and broth composition are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Sup Kambing or generic mutton soup as a food concept in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kambing Soup is approximately 350 ml (350 g), typically served as a main dish at lunch or dinner, often accompanied by crusty roti benggali bread for dipping.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Mutton Soup / Sup Kambing
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Sup Kambing / mutton soup. Values are consistent with the input record (data_source: hpb, provenance: canonical). Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mutton stew (FDC ID 174351) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for low-carbohydrate meat-based soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sup Kambing. GI estimated at 20 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate meat-based soups and broths in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, consistent with a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 8.75) / 100 = 1.75.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg per 100 g; ~1,120 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Kambing Soup due to its high sodium content of approximately 1,120 mg per standard serving, which represents nearly 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; requesting a reduced-salt preparation or consuming a smaller portion is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~20)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kambing Soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; however, the moderate saturated fat content (approximately 3.1 g per 100 g) warrants attention in the context of overall dietary fat management and cardiovascular risk co-management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.1 g per 100 g) and high sodium (320 mg per 100 g) in a single-dish serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Kambing Soup in moderation; the combination of saturated fat (~10.85 g per 350 g serving) and high sodium (~1,120 mg per serving) may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, and portion control or a leaner preparation using less fatty cuts is recommended.

gout.key_factor: High purine content from mutton and marrow bones
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout should avoid or strictly limit Kambing Soup as mutton and bone marrow are high-purine foods that can elevate serum uric acid levels and precipitate acute gout attacks.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kambing Soup (Sup Kambing) is a robust and aromatic Malay and Mamak mutton soup widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore. It is prepared by slow-simmering mutton pieces — typically ribs and marrow bones — in a richly spiced broth made with ginger, garlic, shallots, fennel, cumin, coriander, star anise, and cinnamon. The resulting broth is thick, deeply flavoured, and slightly oily from the rendered mutton fat. The soup is traditionally garnished with fried shallots and chopped Chinese celery, and served with slices of crusty roti benggali (Bengali bread) for dipping. Nutritionally, Kambing Soup provides approximately 110 kcal per 100 g, with 7.5 g protein, 8.2 g fat (of which approximately 3.1 g is saturated), and only 2.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low glycemic index (~20). It is a meaningful source of zinc (3.2 mg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), iron (1.5 mg), and niacin (3.5 mg) per 100 g. However, its sodium content is high at 320 mg per 100 g (~1,120 mg per standard 350 ml serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal and suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. It is commonly consumed as a main meal at lunch or dinner, and is considered a restorative and comforting food in Malay culinary tradition.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sup kambing?
Is mutton soup good for diabetics?
Is sup kambing high in sodium?
Can I eat kambing soup on a keto diet?
What spices are in sup kambing?
Nutritional value of mutton soup Singapore

ingredient_synonyms.kambing: goat / mutton (in Malay, 'kambing' refers to both goat and sheep)
ingredient_synonyms.roti benggali: Bengali bread / crusty white bread loaf
ingredient_synonyms.Chinese celery: daun sup (Malay) / 芹菜 (Chinese) — a more pungent variety of celery used as garnish

confidence_reason: Nutritional values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (data_source: hpb, provenance: canonical, review_status: reviewed, confidence: 0.95 in input). Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy mutton stew entry as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for low-carbohydrate meat-based soups. Overall confidence is high given the canonical HPB provenance for core macros.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture data for Sup Kambing is not disaggregated in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174351) for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16; no approved source reports a water content value for this dish. Saturated fat (3.1 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.4 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g), and cholesterol (28.0 mg) per 100 g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mutton stew (FDC ID 174351) as HPB does not publish fatty acid fractions for this dish; these values are adapted estimates. GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat-based soups in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sup Kambing was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving = (20 × 8.75 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 1.75. FoodEx2 code A0CKM (mutton, cooked) is used as the closest defensible anchor; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for Sup Kambing. The input record cited 'Malaysian Journal of Nutrition / nutriweb.org.my' as a source; this source is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with HPB Singapore (primary) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fatty acid fractions) per Project Veritas source policy. All core macros (protein_g: 7.5, fat_g: 8.2, carbohydrate_g: 2.5) are non-zero and well-supported; no zero-macro justification required.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.4), sodium_mg (320.0), saturated_fat_g (3.1), sugar_g (0.5) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (20), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (1.75), gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database framework, documented in source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI and label present (mutton soup, exact match). FoodEx2 code A0CKM used as closest_parent anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.
